data.title: LCLG: 12 Years Of High Fees And Poor Returns For This Innovative Growth ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article explains why I've assigned a "sell" rating for the Logan Capital Broad Innovative Growth ETF (NYSEARCA:LCLG), an actively managed fund with a 0.96% expense ratio and a long track record of underperforming its benchmark, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 Growth ETF (IWF). Making matters worse is how its components are highly leveraged and don't offer the same growth potential as most low-cost alternatives. I'll highlight a few below for you to consider, and as always, I look forward to your comments afterward.

LCLG Overview

Performance Snapshot vs. Peers

Per its website, LCLG is benchmarked against the Russell 1000 Growth Index. IWF is the most popular ETF tracking this Index, but Vanguard's VONG offers a cheaper expense ratio (0.08% vs. 0.19%), and for these purposes, they're essentially the same fund. In this analysis, I will also evaluate LCLG against the Schwab U.S. Large Cap Growth ETF (SCHG), the SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 Growth ETF (SPYG), and the American Century U.S. Quality Growth ETF (QGRO), as profiled below.

LCLG's inception date is listed as June 28, 2012, but it only launched as an ETF on August 8, 2022, though it was permitted to retain the performance of its predecessor fund for the ten years prior due to the two having "substantially similar investment strategies." You might have noticed its 0.96% expense ratio sticks out for all the wrong reasons, and this is LCLG's first red flag. While it's certainly possible, an active manager can outperform a passive Index, shareholders wind up losing 13.66% of their gains to fees over ten years, assuming a 10% annual return. In contrast, you'll lose only 1.18% with VONG and 0.59% with SCHG and SPYG, according to this calculator.

This is a huge eight-ball that Logan Capital has placed its shareholders behind, and unfortunately, they have not delivered good results. As shown below, LCLG's total return since its inception was 94% worse than IWF (577% vs. 671%) and lagged behind SCHG, the most aggressive growth fund in this sample, by a massive 134%.

Importantly, the fee impact tells only part of the story. The fact that these differences are so huge indicates a problem with the overall strategy, whether that be poor stock selection or underweighting high-potential stocks in the name of improving diversification. I'll explore potential reasons for this shortly, but first, please consider the statistics I calculated based on the graph above compared to IWF.

  • LCLG underperformed by 1.18% on average between 2013 and 2024.
  • LCLG's best year was 2019, outperforming IWF by 3.04%.
  • LCLG's worst year was 2014, underperforming IWF by 7.61%.
  • LCLG's monthly win rate was 54.60% (83/152 months).
  • LCLG outperformed by 1.20% on average in winning months.
  • LCLG underperformed by 1.53% on average in losing months.
  • LCLG's standard deviation was 2.15% higher (17.80% vs. 15.64%)

These statistics paint a picture of an ETF that tends to select above-average risk stocks that can get into trouble in down markets. Things are fine in up marks, as LCLG has a solid 54.60% monthly win rate over IWF, but its high volatility can wipe out those gains quickly and make recovery more difficult. For example, LCLG lost 33.70% in the first nine months of 2022, which required a 50.83% gain just to break even. In contrast, IWF's 30.75% decline needed a 44.44% gain to break even, or 6.39% less. This is why it's important investors consider risk even for growth ETFs.

LCLG Analysis

Strategy Discussion

LCLG follows a proprietary strategy, but its website and prospectus provide some details about how its managers go about selecting high-potential "innovative" growth stocks. I've summarized the most important points from these sources below.

1. The target count is approximately 40-60 U.S. securities with a minimum $5 billion market capitalization selected from a universe of about 1,000.

2. Logan Capital's philosophy is that earnings growth is the most important driver of stock price growth. The firm's goal is to identify companies with "outstanding earnings growth due to factors such as superior pricing power, distribution channels, management, etc."

3. The research process is "bottom-up" and fundamentals-driven, meaning the firm evaluates companies based on their growth prospects, market expansion opportunities, and financial strength. From the website, the strategy "seeks companies that benefit from an economic tailwind" and can "deliver increased earnings tied to pricing power."

4. The firm also relies on macroeconomic statistics like GDP, interest rates, the yield curve, and inflation expectations to aid security analysis.

5. As a double-check, securities must have a strong technical profile that supports the firm's bottom-up research and macroeconomic view.

Overall, the strategy seems quite comprehensive, but since its proprietary, it's difficult to know which factors matter most to the managers. There is also no information provided on the fund's weighting scheme. It's important not to underestimate this feature because, oftentimes, only a handful of stocks drive an ETF's returns. Lately, it's NVIDIA (NVDA), and if you own an ETF that underweights that stock, there's a good chance you've underperformed.

Sector Allocation and Composition Differences vs. Peers

The following table highlights the sector allocation differences between LCLG, IWF, SCHG, SPYG, and QGRO. One aspect that stands out is how LCLG underweights Technology stocks by 9.88% on average, with the offset primarily in Consumer Discretionary. This composition also indicates how the portfolio is pretty aggressive. There is very little allocated to defensive sectors like Consumer Staples, Health Care, and Utilities.

LCLG's top holdings include AppLovin (APP), Netflix (NFLX), and Broadcom (AVGO), and its top 25 holdings comprise 77.37% of the portfolio. As I mentioned earlier, I don't know the fund's weighting scheme, but I'm pretty certain managers just don't rebalance very often. According to my records, AppLovin was LCLG's #23 holding with a 1.53% allocation, but it's now the fund's top holding due to its 420% six-month price return.

Fundamentals By Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for LCLG's top 25 holdings. I've also included summary metrics for IWF, SCHG, SPYG, and QGRO at the bottom.

Here are four key takeaways:

1. LCLG's weighted average market cap is $610 billion, but it has a wide range of sizes, consistent with the $5 billion minimum mentioned earlier. Mega-cap stocks like Apple (AAPL) and Meta Platforms (META) are complimented with small/mid-cap stocks like DICK'S Sporting Goods (DKS) and Lithia Motors (LAD), and while the weighted average market is still quite high, there's an argument to be made that LCLG is an all-cap growth fund. The same is true for QGRO, which allocates 30% to stocks with market caps below $50 billion.

2. LCLG has a weighted average 1.32 five-year beta, and based on its high historical one-year beta and high standard deviation, it's safe to say LCLG is an above-average risky fund.

3. Recall how LCLG's strategy emphasizes growth, but that's tough to reconcile with the figures presented in the table. I looked at three-year historical and one-year estimated sales and earnings per share growth rates, and in all cases, LCLG's rates trail its peers by 2-6%. There are several stocks with mid-single-digit estimated earnings growth rates, like United Rentals (URI), Fastenal (FAST), and Old Dominion Freight Line (ODFL). Their historical rates aren't much better, and as a whole, LCLG's sector-adjusted growth score, which I derived from individual Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, is the weakest of the five, as follows:

  • LCLG: 5.67/10
  • IWF: 5.77/10
  • SCHG: 5.93/10
  • SPYG: 5.96/10
  • QGRO: 6.35/10

While LCLG's 27.65x forward P/E ratio is the most attractive, investors should consider QGRO as a lower-cost alternative. Its growth rates are much better, its forward P/E ratio is nearly the same at 28.84x, it's well-diversified, and, as we'll see next, its quality features are just as robust.

4. LCLG's selections, coincidentally, have the exact same 35.46% weighted average return on equity as IWF. However, their path to these high margins requires a closer look. Consider how a company's return on equity can be broken down into three parts using the DuPont Formula, as follows:

  • Net Income / Revenue (Net Margin)
  • Revenue / Average Assets (Asset Turnover)
  • Average Assets / Average Equity (Financial Leverage)

After canceling out some terms, we're left with Net Income / Average Equity, which is the formula for ROE. This breakdown allows us to see that companies can improve profits through leverage, and that's exactly the case for LCLG. Its selections have a weighted average 1.29 debt-equity ratio compared to 1.09 for IWF and less for the three others. This ratio is not quite the same as the leverage calculation above, but all you need to do is add one to the value to get a good approximation (e.g., 1.29 + 1 = 2.29), so it's still informative.

I also noticed LCLG's weighted average net margins are only 21.45% compared to 25.57% for IWF, so it's undoubtedly more dependent on debt and more sensitive to changes in interest rates. It's concerning, given how, as reported by Seeking Alpha, "year-ahead inflation expectations jumped up from 3.3% last month to 4.3% this month, the highest reading since November 2023 and marking two consecutive months of unusually large increases."

Overall, we see that LCLG's 9.27/10 sector-adjusted profit score is only marginally better than QGRO's 9.24/10 score and well behind IWF, SCHG, and SPYG. As noted in the earlier point, when you consider all that LCLG offers (growth, value, quality, diversification from mega-caps), QGRO simply does it better for a lower cost and less risk. That's also been the case since its launch on September 10, 2018.

Investment Recommendation

LCLG and its predecessor mutual fund have lagged their benchmark by 1.18% on average per year since 2013, with greater volatility. To be fair, this was partly due to lesser allocations in mega-cap stocks in favor of some small-cap growth names. However, my analysis revealed that LCLG's selections have greater financial leverage than its peers, and QGRO is the superior choice for those looking to save on fees while diversifying away from the mega-caps. As a result, I've assigned LCLG a "sell" rating. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway Unlikely To Beat Market In 2025: No Buybacks, No Big Plays
data.description:
I downgraded NYSE:BRK.B stock to "Hold" in early 2024, arguing that despite Berkshire Hathaway's (NYSE:BRK.A) strong operating performance, Buffett’s capital allocation strategy has been too conservative for my liking, with Berkshire being a net seller of equities for multiple quarter, missing a major market rally, while the conglomerate's cash position is constantly swelling to new record heights. With that said, since I have shifted to a neutral position on BRK, shares have started to underperform against the broader U.S. equity markets. For the trailing twelve months, BRK stock is up only about 17%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) gained 21%, respectively.

Now, looking at 2025, I don't think BRK shares can shift gears to outperform again, as Buffett continues to reduce exposure to equities and avoids buybacks of his own stock amidst an arguably rich valuation.

BRK Delivers Solid Operating Performance

Q4 was relatively good for Berkshire Hathaway, from an operating perspective: During the period spanning from October through end of December, Berkshire generated $94.9 billion of revenue up 2% YoY, and very much in line with consensus estimates, according to data collected by Refinitiv. During the same period, operating income jumped to $14.5 billion for the quarter, a notable 71% YoY increase compared to Q4 2023. Strong results were mostly supported by the insurance business, with operating earnings in the underwriting and investment income up 400% YoY (3.4 billion) and 48% YoY ($4.1 billion), respectively. On that note, Buffett commented:

In general, property-casualty (“P/C”) insurance pricing strengthened during 2024, reflecting a major increase in damage from convective storms. Climate change may have been announcing its arrival. However, no “monster” event occurred during 2024. Someday, any day, a truly staggering insurance loss will occur – and there is no guarantee that there will be only one per annum.

At the same time, Berkshire's operating performance was supported by a large gain of income from the conglomerate's large and growing Treasury investments. The railroad business and the energy business performed relatively stable YoY, bringing in operating earnings of about $1.29 billion and $0.3 billion, respectively.

Another Quarter Of Reducing Equity Exposure

As I highlighted in the introduction of this article, I have been concerned about Buffett's decision to reduce exposure to equities in this roaring bull market. In Q4, Buffett's risk appetite has not changed. According to the Q4 print, Buffett sold $143.4 billion worth of stock in 2024, while purchases were only $9.2 billion. With this, 2024 marked an acceleration in Buffett's selling activity compared to 2023, when he sold $40 billion worth of stocks and about $16.5 billion. For reference, in both 2023 and 2024 the S&P 500 scored 20% YoY price appreciation gains.

Personally, I don't think Buffett's cautious approach towards equities will be successful in 2025. In fact, heading into the new year, I wrote an article titled "stocks may melt up like it is 1999" (but I may be wrong, of course):

Wrapping it up, the S&P 500 has crushed it with massive gains in 2023 and 2024, and while some market participants are projecting a more muted 5% upside in 2025, I’m not convinced the rally is over. If anything, the AI boom feels a lot like the internet explosion of the 1990s—but even bigger, thanks to AI’s ability to supercharge margins and productivity across nearly every industry. With top holdings set for 18% revenue growth and political tailwinds like deregulation and tax cuts adding fuel, there’s a strong case for earnings growth outpacing revenues.

I argued that applying a 24-27x multiple on the $280 EPS estimate for the S&P 500, according to data collected by Refinitiv, the implied fair valuation for the S&P 500 should be around 6700-7500 (notable upside of 13-27%).

I am also concerned that Buffett has decided not to buy its own stock (share repurchases either). For FY, share repurchases totaled $2.9 billion, with no incremental buybacks in Q4. Historically, Buffett has been very clear in his communication to investors that his philosophy towards buybacks is very value-driven - he won't buy back shares when he thinks the valuation is stretched.

My Top 3 Takeaways From The Letter

As Buffett always does at the end of the year, he wrote a letter to shareholders. For me, three big lessons stand out. First, own your mistakes and fix them fast. Buffett openly admits he’s made bad investment calls and hiring mistakes, but what really hurts is delaying action—what Charlie Munger called "thumb-sucking." Investors often cling to bad stocks, hoping they’ll bounce back, instead of cutting their losses and moving on. Second, great leadership beats fancy credentials. Buffett couldn’t care less where someone went to school—what matters is whether they can run a business well. Pete Liegl, the RV king who built Forest River into a massive success, didn’t have an Ivy League degree, but his instincts and work ethic made Berkshire billions. Investors should focus on strong management and business fundamentals, not just impressive resumes. Finally, a few big winners make all the difference. Buffett reminds us that "a single winning decision can make a breathtaking difference over time." GEICO, Ajit Jain, and even his lifelong partnership with Munger prove that getting just a handful of things right can outweigh dozens of mistakes. For investors, the message is clear: don’t dwell on small losses, focus on finding great businesses, and let compounding do the heavy lifting.

Valuation: Looks Overvalued

Zooming in on estimates for Berkshire Hathaway, I use the consensus EPS forecast to build a residual earnings valuation model. With projected EPS for FY 2025, FY 2026, and FY 2027 at approximately $20.1, $21, and $21.9, respectively—combined with an 8% cost of equity and a 2.0% terminal growth rate—I estimate Berkshire Hathaway fair implied target valuation at around $289, projecting about 40% downside from current levels. With that, I perfectly understand Buffett's reluctance to buy back shares at current levels.

Below also the sensitivity table varying cost of equity (row) and terminal growth (column).

What's The Upside In 2025?

I am not necessarily bearish on BRK stock in 2025. In my view, Buffett still sits on a portfolio of high-quality stocks, which should do well against a solid macro backdrop. At the same time, Berkshire's massive investments in Treasury securities can be considered relatively safe, they offer a solid 5% income, and they give optionality for shifting back to equities in the case of a market drop. That said, however, I am concerned about Berkshire's set up for a change to outperform the S&P 500 in 2025. As I have highlighted here, the fastest growing stocks in the S&P 500 universe are the magnificent seven names, excluding Apple. At the same time, and as underscored with Q4 results, investors shouldn't expect that share buybacks will juice returns in 2025: In my view, Buffett would likely want to see BRK shares at least ~20% lower before he thinks the valuation set-up is attractive for buybacks (my personal view, anchored also on the residual earnings model presented above). This is not where Berkshire is invested currently. So I don't think that Berkshire is positioned to outperform in 2025.

Investor Takeaway

Berkshire Hathaway delivered a strong Q4 2024, with operating income surging 71% YoY growing to $14.5 billion, driven by strong operating gains in insurance underwriting ($3.4 billion, +400% YoY) and investment income ($4.1 billion, +48% YoY). Despite the solid macro backdrop, Buffett remained cautious on equities: For FY 2024, Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway dumped $143.4 billion in stocks while purchasing just $9.2 billion, an acceleration of his defensive stance from 2023. With no Q4 buybacks and only $2.9 billion repurchased for the year, Buffett signaled that Berkshire's stock may be overvalued. The overvaluation thought is in line with my residual earnings model, which estimates BRK.B's fair implied value at $289 per share. As financial wisdom extracted from the investor letter, I liked how Buffett reinforced 3 key investing principles—admit mistakes quickly, prioritize leadership over credentials, and focus on a few big winners. I remain "Hold" rated.

data.title: Gold: This Is Why Tons Of Gold Are Flowing To New York
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying assets that replicate the price of gold. A unique situation is developing in the global gold market. There is a record increase in inventory in New York, while London, considered the world’s center for gold bullion trading, is facing a severe shortage of the precious metal.

There are several factors that explain this phenomenon, such as tariff concerns, but the reason goes far beyond that and is expected to continue to drive gold prices. Market volatility has been heightened by this growing imbalance, and has opened up opportunities for arbitrage by investors.

Options That Replicate Gold Prices

There are two great ETFs for investors looking for exposure to gold. They are the SPDR Gold Shares (NYSEARCA:GLD) and the iShares Gold Trust (NYSEARCA:IAU). The GLD ETF has a 0.40% management fee and $80.9 billion in assets under management, while the IAU ETF has a 0.25% management fee and $36.9 billion in assets under management.

In my opinion, if you are a retail investor, you should choose the IAU because of its more attractive pricing and lower management fee. GLD, on the other hand, has a higher management fee but is more liquid, and is recommended for institutional investors.

Unusual Situation

There is an unusual situation happening in the market. London, known as the world’s gold trading hub, is experiencing a severe shortage of the metal, which has caused major delays in gold withdrawals from vaults. Meanwhile, New York is seeing gold stocks reach record levels. Analysts say this is due to fears of the Trump administration imposing tariffs. Let's take a closer look at this.

The Tariff Hypothesis

It is intriguing to think that the Trump administration’s tariff policies could be responsible for this market imbalance. The US is only the world’s fifth-largest producer of gold, and its tariffs would probably not be as effective as tariffs on steel, aluminum and oil.

It is also important to remember that while commodities such as steel and aluminum may have an incentive for domestic production due to the trade war, gold has different fundamentals.

This precious metal is a finite resource and cannot be increased or replaced. Furthermore, it is not an essential input, but rather a financial asset. That is why I do not believe in the tariff hypothesis, but rather in deeper hypotheses.

Most Plausible Hypothesis

In my view, a more plausible idea for this discrepancy is a joint movement by the BRICS, led by China, of massive gold purchases. In 2023 alone, central banks led by China bought 1,037 metric tons of gold, and the Chinese Central Bank bought more than all the others combined.

It’s interesting when you look deeper into the data. China has been dumping its US Treasury holdings since 2015, and its gold purchases have been growing at the same rate. This says a lot about its long-term financial goals.

It is quite possible that the Chinese administration has seen the Western sanctions on Russia, and is trying to reduce dependence on the Western financial system amid its goals of annexing the island of Taiwan.

Could the flow of physical gold from London to New York be a move aimed at limiting the acquisition of gold by China and the BRICS countries? I have no sources to corroborate this idea, but it is an assumption based on the information I will provide below.

Coordinated Movement?

There were recent reports that after the US presidential election, around 393 metric tons of gold were transferred to the COMEX (Commodity Exchange Inc.) vault in New York. This move alone saw a 75% increase in stockpiles, with HSBC and J.P. Morgan leading the charge.

Fair Price

As we know, gold does not provide cash flow like a company, which makes it difficult to value. Therefore, we need to understand other variables. Currently, we have massive purchases by central banks and the possible continuation of interest rate cuts by the FED.

In addition, gold is being used as a geopolitical hedge, and US fiscal risks add more risk. With this, my projections point to the possibility of prices reaching $3,100/oz by the end of 2025.

Assuming that gold reaches my target, we have an upside of 7.1% considering current prices. This corroborates my recommendation to buy assets that replicate the price of gold, since it is an asset for times of instability.

I also feel more confident about the recommendation given that Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend buying ETFs like the SPDR® Gold Shares ETF and iShares Gold Trust ETF.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

There is a great risk to the bullish thesis on gold, and this risk involves the reduction of geopolitical tensions. But how could this happen? We need to analyze it in depth, and I will use the example of the Cold War.

According to many experts, the Cold War did not evolve into a new World War because the Soviet Union collapsed financially. We need to consider that something similar could happen again.

There is a clear antagonism between the West (led by the US) and the East (led by China). If China collapses financially (there are signs of economic weakening), it would consequently not have the strength to threaten American supremacy in the world.

President Trump has already threatened the members of the BRICS if they continue with plans to replace the dollar as the trading currency. In my view, the great risk to the gold thesis lies in the success of the Trump administration.

The Bottom Line

A curious situation has occurred since President Trump's election: there has been a massive flow of gold leaving London and heading to New York. While many investors believe that the reason is fear of tariffs, we have seen that this theory does not make much sense.

On the other hand, the theory that Eastern countries led by China are seeking to reduce their dependence on Western financial power is much more plausible and generates fear among investors.

Based on this analysis, I recommend buying assets that replicate the price of gold. As long as investors remain skeptical about this turbulent geopolitical scenario, the flow of investments linked to gold will continue to grow.

data.title: MeridianLink: Capital Allocation Is The Question
data.description:
Introduction

I started looking at MeridianLink (NYSE:MLNK) back in 2021 when they IPO’d, and then bought some shares in 2022. I didn’t hold them for too long though with growing fears around end-market housing demand (which impacts their mortgage volumes). But between housing seemingly a little more stable today alongside investments in the interim, I thought it’d be worthwhile to swing back around to see if there’s any worthwhile investment case. Summarily, while I really like the business and expect the upcoming Q4 to produce another quarter of roughly mid-single-digit organic growth, I’m just not sure there’s enough upside yet at today’s price in the high-teens.

Sales: Long-Term Tailwinds

Q3 consolidated sales came in at $80.4M, which is up (organically) a solid 5% from the ~$76.5M posted in Q3 last year. By segment, their Lending Software business was up 7% to ~$63M, and their Data Verification business down just marginally to $17.4M. On a 9-month basis, sales were ~$237M, up ~3.4% from ~$229M posted last year, so they’ve seen growth throughout the year. However, by segment again, Lending was up 7% again to ~$185.6M, while Data Verification was down 9% to ~$51.3M. In both periods, their subscription fees were healthy growers too.

From the top, they confirm that pricing was a low-single-digit contributor to sales growth, which frankly isn’t very surprising. They’ve talked about this before in prior calls, but MLNK includes a price escalation clause in their contracts, which apparently, has resulted in about a 3-4-5% price increase historically. Thus, with sales up 5%, the residual growth is volume driven, implying either (1) more lending volumes per existing client versus last year or (2) more clients.

Digging into Lending’s 7% sales growth which includes probably 3-4% volume growth, let’s start by thinking about this from a volume per existing customer angle. From what I can gather, this was probably a net negative all considered. On the mortgage side, some data I’ve found suggests that mortgage originations were actually up marginally in Q3. However, they themselves note that while “[m]ortgage volumes in the quarter were strong year-over-year,” “...the majority of our contracts are at or below their committed minimums…” Indeed, Lending mortgage revenues were down 9%, and Data Verification mortgage revenues were down 2%.

Furthermore, per this data, market-wide credit card originations were also down materially in Q3. Although, per the Q2 call, they suggest this actually hasn’t been as impactful to them, although still perhaps negative. With respect to their pre-owned auto exposure - a material component - broader loan originations were about flat year-over-year. And consistent with this, while they do recognize the near-term market headwinds, they’re not really suggesting that auto has been too much of a drag on results here either. Also, HELOC loan balances have been increasing lately, as consumers turn to untapped credit availability in today’s market.

But all considered, with mortgage being lower and auto seemingly being at least not higher, I tend to contextualize their “same-customer-volumes” as being lower year-over-year. Thus, the implied 3-4% volume growth estimate for Lending is seemingly a mix of lower volumes being more than offset by new logo and/or cross-selling growth.

From the top, we know that churn was a low-single-digit headwind, which frankly is not all that much and demonstrates the overall stickiness of their solutions. I don’t really contextualize this to be any more than natural period-to-period, not structural declines. Indeed, from the call, they run down a list of recent wins, citing, for example, a cross sell win from a large ($750M AUM) credit union adding MLNK’s mortgage platform alongside other various add-ons. And they also noted Broadway Bank - a $5.5B AUM bank in Texas - who went live in the quarter with MeridianLink Mortgage. Another new logo win was a credit union for whom they displaced an existing provider - the solution was not mortgage related but for consumer lending.

To this end, if I was to further disaggregate the wins, I’d actually expect most of them to be share gains. From a market-angle, there’s nothing really there to suggest much growth. Actually, it should be noted that the number of credit unions have been declining every year - which might explain some of the churn - so it’s hard to imagine any new logo wins from this dynamic. And we can see that general marketplace consolidation has resulted in a declining number of banks over time too. So, while I don’t have perfect data on all financial institutions and their respective trends, it’s conceivable that the broader landscape is following a similar trend and has actually declined versus last year.

But then the second market-related point is that, at this stage, all FIs obviously have some type of software/lending solution, so MLNK isn’t finding untapped customers. They confirm this too by citing that their historical wins “...tend to replace legacy solutions, point-to-point solutions and even in a rare instance at the real lower end, something that's even more manual than that.” This then is why I believe their recent wins are share-driven and not market.

Furthermore, I think they’re largely structural too as the growth Lending is seeing is, I believe, a time and scale dynamic - that is, from growth in their customer coverage. The quick historical context is this: from pre-COVID up until today, they’ve essentially taken a non-existent sales department to now an expanded, professional one. That is, they’ve gone from a few reps pre-COVID to over 50 or so today - were previously handling just inbound inquiries - and that investment has carried over in the past 12 months with S&M expenses up 11% (more than sales). They’ve continued to build out their team (hire reps) to fully professionalize and organize the department how they want with seemingly a heavier focus on optimizing their “gatherers” (customer success) team. Thus, when I contextualize the past 12 months, I can imagine that increased efforts here are contributing to their recent wins.

What’s implicitly happening then is that MLNK’s customers are seeing better value in their solutions. To this end, I think this goes to reflect that MLNK end-to-end solution offering is indeed superior and going to beat point solutions over time - not only is MLNK probably going to offer volume discounts, but it would seemingly make the life of the FI a little easier, particularly as the number of system integration grows. This would then be consistent with their historical comments and growth too.

On the Data Verification side of the business, the dynamics aren’t different directionally, but the magnitudes are. That is, here too, price was likely positive, implying volume declines in the mid-single-digit territory. But slightly different from their Lending business, more than half of their sales are mortgage-driven. Thus, consistent with the aforementioned mortgage headwinds for Lending, Data Verification is also seeing a headwind here with management citing a “...onetime downsell of a single large customer, which is now operating at their minimum for the remainder of the year.”

This is a tougher business to get real-time insight into because it’s smaller and more niche. If we think about the context here, this business grew materially in 2021, then declined in 2022, 2023, and now here in 2024. This is a somewhat concerning trend, but not as bad as it may appear. That is, part of the 2021 growth came from the annualization of their TazWorks acquisition in 2020 alongside mortgage originations growth. But consistent with the rest of the market thereafter, mortgage originations have declined since 2022 onwards.

So, from one angle, this business has more or less been tracking market trends, which is seemingly consistent with what I believe to be the case today. At the same time, however - also consistent with what I’m gathering today - there is not much of an offsetting growth story. There is some customer overlap where MLNK can probably cross-sell these solutions, but it’s not full customer overlap. So, when I think about today’s sales trends, I tend to just attribute the decline to market-driven volume declines.

Looking forward, today’s sales probably amount to something like $320M-ish on an annual basis - there’s not too much seasonality - slightly higher than their $315M they’re guiding for here in FY24.

With Q4 coming around, I expect them to produce a relatively similar result to what they've posted all year, particularly as pricing mechanically continues. Given the near-term macro context, we could see some volumes continue to soften in some areas, but like management has pointed out, there’s some general stability all around the business - i.e., not much is rapidly changing anymore. Existing home sales - which will roughly track mortgage originations - have been increasing sequentially in Q4 2024, so this could be a slight tailwind. Coupled with continuing to expand their sales base and we should not see anything materially dramatic compared to Q3 results.

Over time, I’m okay assuming that where we are today market volume-wise is a decent normalized spot too. The big question is whether they’re going to continue winning new Lending clients over time, for which I think the answer is yes. How much and for how long though, I’m not sure just yet. From one angle, we’re sort of nearing the end of their S&M investment and development, so I don’t think we’re going to replicate the past 3-5 years on this. And then from a different angle, there are nonetheless other solid competitors out there, so while they may continue to take some share from point solutions over time, it won’t obviously continue forever. But nonetheless, between today and 10 years from now, I do expect them to house more clients given the structural shift from a value proposition standpoint that competitors will have a hard time catching up on.

Putting it all together then, pricing can probably remain positive in the low-single-digits over time - these solutions are super sticky as their history shows. If per client volumes start to stabilize a little bit to reduce the headwind we’re seeing today for both Lending and Data Verification, and they can maintain their new logo and cross-selling growth from continued salesforce development, 5% doesn’t appear unreasonable for the foreseeable future. All else equal then, this growth will eventually get down to tracking price in the end, but that seems like a long way away in my opinion. So, I’m modeling today’s $320M in sales to growing 5% to FY28 sales of ~$385M.

Margins: Leverage To Capture

With respect to margins, they posted gross margins of 65.6%, up about 100 bps from last year. And on a 9-month basis, gross margins grew about 180 bps, so it’s been positive across the year. From an EBITDA perspective, margins grew ~300 bps to 42.1%. On a 9-month basis though, a la gross margins, they grew 535 bps. So, their margins have been performing well at both levels.

Breaking down their gross margins in the quarter, it would seem to me that they’re benefiting from recent price increases like I talked about earlier - it’s just a question of how much cost inflation offset this. To this end, I’m not sure they’re really pushing through a huge amount of price given the demand context, and we also know from tracking other businesses that wage inflation hasn’t let up. So, I supposed there could be some slight tailwind from this variable, but I can more confidently say that their price/cost basis wasn’t a headwind.

From a different angle, there are going to be some mix impacts worth calling out. That is, with respect to Q3’s results, services revenue grew more than their subscription and other revenue. While they don’t call out the margin differential specifically, if we take a business like Magic Software (MGIC), for instance, we can see that subscription revenue will naturally come in at a materially higher margin. Thus, for MLNK, this mix shift was a slight gross margin headwind, although, considering the size of the mix shift, it was probably less than a 50 bps impact.

Directionally offsetting this, however, would be fixed cost leverage. That is, with sales up 5% year-over-year with maybe half of that being volume, they’re naturally going to get some fixed cost leverage. COGS are going to include various personnel and cloud hosting costs - among other smaller overhead costs - that shouldn’t really scale with sales growth, so qualitatively speaking, fixed cost leverage is a reasonable explanation. On the other hand though, sales weren’t up that much, so it could certainly be the case that there was either some randomness resulting in lower COGS somewhere or price was more positive to margins than I’m inferring. Either way though, I feel confident that the growth is a reflection of those two variables.

Moving down the operating level, however, this fixed cost leverage becomes a little more evident with EBITDA margins up 300 bps versus last year. Indeed, like most businesses, there’s going to be a lot of leverage on the SG&A front with most back-office costs being relatively fixed. But then with respect to their S&M base specifically, you can imagine that the cross-selling deals are coming in at a relatively high incremental as it’s effectively their gross margin minus sales commission - there’s not the relatively higher CAC associated with that sale.

From a cost perspective, if we look at reported G&A and S&M, both were higher year-over-year which was offset by ~$1M of lower R&D, although some of this growth in the other buckets was SBC-driven which gets added back to EBITDA, so reported amounts aren’t a perfect measure. But we know they completed a restructuring in 2023 which resulted in reducing their workforce by 11%. Although, they did push through this plan in early 2023, so at this point, it was probably fully baked in Q3 2023 numbers, so when thinking about the year-over-year comparison, the residual cost benefits are probably relatively little. Still though, there’s seemingly some as they attribute lower R&D expenses to “lower staffing due to our previously announced restructuring.”

On the other hand, we know they’ve been making investments in their sales team as I called out earlier, which they confirm by noting that their S&M expense “increase is due to investment in go-to-market.” On top of that, the G&A expense growth was partly driven by “select discretionary investments made to position the company for scale in 2025 and beyond.” What those investments are, I’m not sure, but I think this goes on to confirm that on a net basis, the year-over-year operating leverage was more sales driven as the cost side of the equation seems to have been an offset between residual savings and investments.

Thinking about margins then going forward, from a pricing perspective, I don’t really see there being that much leftover growth here, if any. Gross margins in the high-60% isn’t an abnormal range considering the Services mix and relative to other software businesses I’ve researched. And this is consistent with their history - e.g., in 2019, when they had a similar revenue mix, they posted 69% gross margins. Today’s level then is about 300 bps lower, although my sense is that, while I can’t confirm, this was likely negatively impacted by the acquisitions post-COVID, which, frankly, didn’t carry materially high margins, at least relative to MLNK’s legacy profile. Thus, I’m not contextualizing there to be much room to grow at this point, although I don’t think they’ll go backwards - it’s reasonable for them to track inflation over time, consistent with my modeling input for price growth over time.

From an operating leverage standpoint, however, at this point, I think MLNK is running on a fairly mature and normalized profile. I say this considering where EBITDA margins are in the low-40% range, but also considering the rationalization implemented in 2023 resulting in a reduced workforce. I think if there was a good amount of fat left to cut, they probably would have done so already given the demand environment. Furthermore, I noted this earlier, but I think the outsized S&M investments are going to come down to a more normalized level.

Translating this context into normalized incrementals, the year-over-year incrementals aren’t normalized given that cost savings that this included and S&M investments. However, even if they were to keep investing into their S&M base, something in the 50% range is totally reasonable to me. Historical incrementals don’t really help us out much because of the impact of M&A on margins, but if we think about this qualitatively, for each incremental sale (on average), they should be capturing something like a 70% gross margin minus some percentage of their SG&A profile. If we just assumed that half of SG&A scaled, that’d imply about mid-50% incremental EBITDA margins. I feel okay modeling this going forward, although I do want to remain cautious of the ongoing investments, so I’m going to model it at 50%.

I’m not expecting material mix impacts going forward - Services should more or less track subscription over time given their natural relationship - so what that means then is that if pricing turns out to be immaterial going forward in terms of it’s margin impact relative to cost growth, and they’re able to capture 50% incremental EBITDA margins, should sales grow to $385M in FY28, that’d imply EBITDA of ~$165 (~43% margins) relative to the $320M in sales and $135M in EBITDA they’re tracking at today.

Valuation

Rounding out this discussion, let’s talk capital allocation, because this is a debatable variable in the model. I’m comfortable modeling capex (again, including capitalized software costs) at 3.25%, which is admittedly just slightly lower than what they’ve been running at. However, as they grow, they should capture growing capex leverage as those dollars can get spread over more customers.

As for the rest of their dollars, however, it’s not as simple. Year-to-date, but they’ve spent an overwhelming amount of their FCF lately on share repurchases, spending just over $100M thus far this year (versus ~$36M last year). And in FY23, it was the same when they spent ~$61M on repurchases as well. If this was the entire, I’d be quite attracted.

However, pre-FY23 has seen a different story. Minus some one-time transactions pertaining to their IPO, the vast majority of their FCF dollars were spent on acquisitions. In 2021, they spent ~$85M on TazWorks and ~$36M on Saylent, and then ~$23M on StreetShares and ~$62M on OpenClose in 2022, all for a collective spend over those 2 years of ~$206M. For context, their operating cash flow between those two years was just about $165M, or about $150M including capex spend. So, all FCF went to M&A.

This is not fundamentally bad, but the returns seemingly haven’t been exceptional. Strategically, they make sense, but financially, while we don’t have all of the figures, just from a high level, they posted $104M in EBITDA in FY20, and today they’re on pace to post around $130M, so call it $25M in EBITDA growth. Now, the housing market has softened, sure, so this isn’t a perfect way to judge returns, but if we think about something like $25M being generated by the above M&A, that’s an EBITDA multiple of ~8.3x. And that’s an overly aggressive assumption given organic growth. That’s not bad though - that’s an implied NOPAT multiple of around 16x.

However, the data doesn’t quite add up. If I take OpenClose, they acquired them in late 2022, so 2023 captured a full year relative to 1 month in 2022. Yet, EBITDA was flat between 2022 and 2023. Furthermore, OpenClose posted $12M of annual revenue and that their organic sales were flat, so that indeed confirms OpenClose’s EBITDA contribution was minimal. Also, they should’ve captured some benefit from StreetShares too. And furthermore, we they should’ve captured restructuring cost savings. Considering all of this then, their returns don’t reflect anything close to a mid-teens multiple.

I find a lot of this to more or less just be R&D via M&A. Now, do they continue to spend all of their FCF on M&A going forward? Probably not - some of this spend was seemingly growth-oriented - but it’s going to continue at some pace. To this end, I’m going to be probably a little too conservative and assume $20M a year is devoted to M&A with zero return, implying a true capex rate of ~8.5%. This is a perfect model, but this is how I’m accounting for this for now.

Putting the model together, I’m assuming MLNK can post sales/EBITDA of $385M/$165M in FY28. Minus D&A of ~$69M (18% of sales), SBC of ~$39M (10% of sales), interest expense of $45M, taxes at 25%, and then capex of ~$33M (8.5% of sales), I get a bottom line free-cash-flow estimate of $45M.

With ~75.632M diluted shares outstanding and today’s price of ~$19/share, MLNK currently trades at a market cap of ~$1.437B. Current cash stands at ~$82M, and total debt stands at ~$470M.

From that economic profile, even with 5% long-term growth, the IRR isn’t more than high-single-digits. However, obviously, if they don’t spend money on M&A and capex runs at a true 3.25%, they’d be posting FCF of $65M and the IRR would be materially higher at around 9%, which certainly changes the investment decision. Maybe they don’t spend as much as I’m modeling and the IRR is somewhere in the 8% range in the middle there. That’s a reasonable possibility, I think. However, I wouldn’t be comfortable with modeling mid-single-digit growth over time and ~3.25% capex intensity.

So, I like the growth prospects of MLNK - there’s no doubt on that front. They can keep pushing through price and cross-selling over time and continue winning new logos. It’s a great growth story there. But the capital allocation concerns are front and center, and I’m not sure there’s enough evidence yet where I can make a more precise quantitative judgement (something I partly overlooked last time I owned them).

Conclusion

All told, I really like MLNK and it’s business - I think they have a fairly durable competitive position and will see growth over time. However, an investment requires the right price too, not just a good business, and to this end, I’m just not sure we’re there yet. I may be too chintzy over a few dollars, but I’m trying to remain disciplined in my process.

data.title: Vipshop: Fundamental Challenges Remain
data.description:
Vipshop (NYSE:VIPS) reported 4Q24 results with revenue beating consensus as GMV came in better than expected. This resulted in non-GAAP operating profit and net income ahead of consensus. Management guidance for 1Q25 was also slightly ahead of consensus. The share traded up post the print, but we remain cautious on the stock given the headline numbers mask the ongoing weakness and deterioration of fundamentals of the firm.

Our thesis on VIPS is that this stock has been a paradox of weak fundamental factors coupled with attractive financial metrics. On one hand, VIPS has been able to deliver consistent free cash and ROIC, which makes its low single-digit P/E multiple highly appealing to value investors. On the other hand, the deteriorating fundamentals in operating metrics, questionable sustainability, and platform relevancy remain key risks to the company's long-term growth.

While 4Q24 results continue to show positive headline numbers, these do not change the weak narrative. Similar trends were evident last quarter and this quarter does not appear to have material improvement.

A few areas of concern:

Operating metrics remain weak

Although total revenue was above consensus, GMV continued to see little or no growth. GMV was largely flat, down 0.3% year over year, while results from Alibaba (BABA) indicated a more rational competitive environment in which BABA saw GMV improvement in its Taobao and Tmall groups.

More concerning was VIPS continued to see user churn, with the number of active buyers decreasing by 5.8% y/y to 45.7m, lower than the consensus estimate of 46m, while the number of orders also declined by 7.2% y/y to 217.5m.

While the bull may argue that the per-order and per-customer metrics remain healthy on a y/y basis, we would argue that this is simply a function of a smaller denominator and does not represent operating leverage.

On the other hand, Super VIP membership saw 50% growth y/y in the quarter and contributed to 51% of the online spending. The total number of SVIP members reached 8.8m for the full year, accounting for 49% of the total sales, implying that SVIP momentum was solid in the Q. However, overreliance on the SVIP to drive GMV growth is problematic given that VIPS could be focused on a smaller segment of its users, deploying resources (i.e., Unlimited returns) to cater to their needs while underserving the larger consumer base.

Overleveraging on the appliance category

Management indicated that consumers were cautious about their discretionary spending, and VIPS was able to capture this value-for-money mentality within the apparel segment, which saw 2% growth from a year ago and now accounts for 75% of the total GMV.

For the non-apparel categories, management cited that they only accounted for RMB 300mn to RMB400mn for home appliances and digital products in Q4. We were disappointed to see this, given that China has rolled out a large-scale subsidy program for digital products and home appliances. JD is a key beneficiary of this, given that home appliances and electronics account for over half of its revenue.

The GMV concentration on apparel can be a double-edged sword, given that no single e-commerce platform can dominate this category due to a broad consumer preference, but the return rate for apparel is also high which could result in some volatility in the GMV metric due to shift in consumer preference or seasonality impact.

Lack of global exposure vs. the other e-commerce peers

VIPS has a smaller global exposure than its peers PDD, Alibaba, Shein, and ByteDance. Although we see VIPS to be a value trap and questionable relevance in China's e-commerce landscape, VIPS's business model of driving internet penetration of discount designer products still has a value proposition in markets outside of China where this category has lower internet penetration and presents VIPS with an attractive opportunity to address this gap similar to how PDD addressed the discount e-commerce gap with the introduction of Temu.

We believe VIPS should allocate its cash better by investing in the overseas market, where it could achieve higher growth potential than China. Both PDD and Meituan have leveraged their domestic expertise to expand overseas successfully, and we feel that VIPS can deliver greater shareholder value by following similar footsteps.

In terms of valuation, the current multiple remains compelling relative to peers at less than 6x forward earnings compared with PDD and JD, which trade between 9x and 10x, and BABA, which trades at 13.5x. However, we would caution that VIPS is valued at this depressed multiple due to its lack of GMV trajectory and is best avoided until a stable GMV recovery is evident.

In the meantime, investors are better off with ecommerce plays with global exposure and our preference is for PDD and Meituan.

data.title: IEUR: European Equities Are Going To Be Great Again!
data.description:
In September last year, former European Central Bank governor Mario Draghi published a report on European Union competitiveness. I've was bullish on Europe following that. I wrote about several investment opportunities including Euronext N.V. (OTCPK:EUXTF) here, the SPDR® EURO STOXX 50 ETF (FEZ) here, the iShares MSCI Italy ETF (EWI) here, and the iShares MSCI Spain ETF (EWP) here. But I have found another potentially better investment to play Europe's turnaround and recovery: the iShares Core MSCI Europe ETF (NYSEARCA:IEUR).

I will get into the details of IEUR in a moment, but first let's reconsider the pros and cons of investing in Europe right now.

First the argument against. There's a strong view that only the G2 matters now and that they are pulling ahead: USA and China. They lead in the crucial areas of innovation, economic size, military and global power and only these two countries have interesting investment opportunities for the medium and long term. Indeed, Daniel Lacalle the respected economist and fund manager wrote following Draghi's report that will likely fail and that the European Union could become "the world's museum and a wasteland of zombie businesses". Yes, Europe has a lot of historical sites and is a great tourist destination, and yes business regulation in Europe is too high right now, and costs have made Europe uncompetitive in many areas.

But that is changing. Trump's election has been a wake up call for European policymakers. The European Union is starting to roll back regulation, recognising the competitive disadvantage high regulation has created against the United States and China. At the recent World Economic Forum at Davos, European policymakers pushed back against the idea that Europe would become the world's museum. French President Emmanuel Macron unveiled a new and credible plan to "Make France an AI powerhouse". Forget "drill, baby, drill" Macron said, it's all about "plug, baby, plug" referring to plugging in data centers to France's nuclear powered electricity grid.

Ursula von der Leyen's DOGE and German's elections

Ursula von der Leyen is busy undoing the green agenda of her last term. Her push to simplify EU [European Union] rules happened so fast that the policy world in Brussels did not really know about it until it was already happening.

Source: Eurointelligence.com, 19 February 2025

Then there is the German election. The centre-left Social Democratic Party leadership of Europe's largest economy has been a disaster. It is well worth reading Kaput: The End of the German Miracle by the respected commentator on European affairs Wolfgang Münchau, to learn more about the structural issues facing Germany. But the German elections on 23 February have brought to power the centre-right Christian Democratic Union led by Friedrich Merz. According to a recent profile of him in the BBC, Mr Merz took a break from politics in starting in 2009 and forged "a career in finance and corporate law – becoming a boardroom executive at various international firms and, reputedly, a millionaire." CNBC notes that Merz served for a while as the chairman of Blackrock (BLK) Germany’s supervisory board. So Merz is a business friendly leader, and Germany is Europe's most important economy - so for me this is a very positive development.

Next, I see that the European Central Bank is starting to talk sense now. In a recent interview with the Financial Times, Isabel Schnabel, a Member of the Executive Board of the ECB signalled that policy will be driven again by data dependence and that "we are getting closer to the point where we may have to pause or halt our rate cuts". This will be good to keep inflation under control and stable interest rates will be good for industrials and financials.

Finally, the big macro event I can predict, is that an end or freeze to the Russia-Ukraine war, will eventually bring back cheap Russian gas to Europe's economy. European gas prices have been too high during this war. Economics will beat geopolitics and Europe needs cheap energy to help revive both the consumer and industry. When this happens, natural gas prices will fall from their current high levels of around 50 euros per MegaWatt hour, and Europe will regain some competitiveness in manufacturing.

Why the iShares Core MSCI Europe ETF?

First of all the ETF fundamentals. Seeking Alpha's quant rating is a buy and the ETF grades are A for Expenses and Liquidity, A- for Momentum and Dividends, and B- for Risk. I don't really care about the Risk rating, because to make returns you have to take risk.

From a Technical Analysis point of view, IEUR faces resistance now at $60/share. I think it will break through that and rally hard thereafter.

Holdings

IEUR tracks the MSCI Europe Investable Market Index and currently holds just under 1000 holdings. The details of the ETF can be found on the helpful iShares page. The top 50 stocks, roughly speaking Europe's main large cap stocks, make up about 47% of the portfolio's weight. This is important because that means, again roughly speaking 53% of the IEUR portfolio is in stocks is in mid- and small-capitalization European equities, which over time have tended to give higher returns than large cap stocks. Indeed, the MSCI index factsheet notes that the median stock has a USD market cap of $1.9 billion - so the portfolio is a blend of large, mid and small caps.

In the US we have the Magnificent Seven. In Europe we have the "GRANOLAS" GSK, Roche, ASML, Nestle, Novartis, Novo Nordisk, l'Oreal, LVMH, AstraZeneca, SAP and Sanofi. IEUR holds all these stocks and together these stocks have an 18% weighting in IEUR. Then, as the Financial Times recently wrote Germany's stock market has its own Mag7: SAP, defence stock Rheinmetall, industrial conglomerate Siemens, Siemens Energy, Deutsche Telekom, and insurers Allianz and Munich Re. These seven momentum stocks drove the bulk of the German DAX index's near 20% return in 2024. I wrote a bullish thesis on Siemens Energy back at the end of November.

These seven German stocks make up, ironically, 7% weighting of IEUR. I also recently wrote bullish articles on London listed Anglo American (here) and Glencore (here). If we add up the weights of the innovative GRANOLAS, the German Mag 7 (not double counting SAP), and my bullish Anglo American and Glencore positions, then these top 19 stocks account for a current weighting in IEUR of 23%. This is a strong backbone of the IEUR portfolio.

Holding Exposures

Financials make up 20.6% and Industrials make up 18.4%.

Within Industrials, there are five important defence stocks, which have rallied hard following the 2025 Munich Security Conference, when it became clear that Europe will now be spending billions of dollars more on defence. Along with the aforementioned Germany's Rheinmetall (a big supplier to Ukraine), these are the UK's BAE Systems, France's Thales, Italy's Leonardo and Sweden's Saab. Together these five stocks have a weighting at time of writing of slightly over 1% of the total ETF.

In terms of markets, UK listed stocks make up 23%, France 16.3%, and Germany 14%. Italy, a market I really like now, is just under 5% of the weighting. Switzerland, generally a well run country, largely immune from geopolitical turbulence, is another 14% of the portfolio.

It's also important to mention that IEUR has a very low Expense Ratio of just 0.09% and this is probably because its assets under management are relatively large at over $4 billion. The next lowest Expense Ratio of main peers is 0.45%. So you get to keep more of the returns. Over time this 36 basis point saving adds up to significant amounts.

Valuation

Trailing 12 months dividend yield is 3.2%. The underlying index, MSCI Europe IMI trades on a forward price earnings of 13.8x, compared to MSCI World IMI (which is dominated by US stocks) at forward P/S of 19.2x. The iShares MSCI World ETF (URTH) has a TTM yield of just 1.4%.

Risks

In the medium term, the main risk I can see is if Donald Trump's tariffs induce some kind of global recession, hitting all equity markets globally, including Europe. Other risks include policy reforms getting bogged down in bureaucracy and Europe failing to make improvements on competitiveness. Another risk is that more proactive managements move their listings to the USA, and European equity markets gets stuck with the leftovers. However, many national champions in Europe are unlikely to move their primary listing to the US because of longstanding ties. This risk might apply more to technology companies and global companies with less ties to a European home.

Conclusion

Europe has lagged the US badly in the last 20 years, and probably for good reason. European policymakers are waking up finally, macro fundamentals should improve going forward. Europe does have a backbone of innovative stocks in the GRANOLAS, a handful of important defence suppliers, and the momentum of German equities as the German elections likely bring a left to right political change, meaning a more business friendly environment in Europe's most important economy. The broad portfolio of large, mid and small cap stocks and the low expense ratio mean that IEUR is a good long-term "buy and hold" ETF. The valuation and dividends are attractive relative to the equivalent global option, represented by iShares MSCI World ETF.

Finally, investors whose portfolios are dominated by US stocks might want to add this fund as an efficient way to get global diversification.

Europe might look like a basket case from the outside, but IEUR has rallied from $54 to $59.80 per share in the last four weeks and don't forget it's always darkest before the dawn.

data.title: PennantPark Investment: Dividend Cut May Not Happen After All (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

PennantPark Investment (NYSE:PNNT) operates as a business development company that aims to generate attractive total returns through its portfolio of diverse debt investments. PNNT is a bit more unique compared to other BDCs because of its larger focus on equity-based investments, which may not produce consistent cash flows and show a greater sensitivity to interest rate changes. I previously covered PNNT and issued a sell rating due to a weaker portfolio strength proven by growing non-accruals, an unjustified higher valuation, and weakening distribution coverage. Now that we've crossed into the new year and PNNT reported their Q1 earnings, I wanted to provide an updated look into its portfolio and valuation based on the shifted outlook.

Looking at the performance over the last 12 months, PNNT's price has increased by 7.6%. When including distributions, the total return jumps up to 22.7% over the same period. One of the main appeals of PNNT is that it offers a high dividend yield of about 13.3%. This double-digit yield makes it very attractive for investors seeking a high level of income generation within their portfolio. However, I don't believe that PNNT's dividend is the safest within the BDC sector, as the base net investment income doesn't cover the distribution. As it stands, management is using cash from undistributed spillover to make up the difference in the short term. Assuming earnings stay the same, management can stretch this capital out for two years.

In terms of valuation and price stability, I will admit that PNNT has held up a lot better than expected. The price run has caused the discount to NAV level to shrink considerably. However, I don't necessarily see any indication of strong growth that supports this shrinking discount. Non-accruals at cost haven't shown much improvement and earnings continue to trend downward. While there are some positives in terms of portfolio growth related to PNNT's Joint Venture, I am not fully convinced yet. So let's start by taking a look at PNNT's portfolio strategy.

Portfolio Strategy

PNNT's portfolio currently has a total value of approximately $1.4B, spanning across 158 direct investments. Although the latest quarterly update was reported in February, management releases their portfolio data on a calendar year breakdown, so the latest information is from December 2024. PNNT focuses on making investments into companies that have an EBITDA between $10M to $50M. From a sector focus, PNNT is primarily concentrated between the following investment areas:

  • Healthcare
  • Government Services
  • Software & Technology
  • Consumer
  • Business Services

More importantly, the range of investments is pretty diverse, with an average weight of 0.6% per position across its entire portfolio. First lien senior secured investments make up 49% of the portfolio, which is a decrease from the 58% weight in first lien investments at the start of 2024. This is notable because first lien senior secured investments sit at the top of the corporate capital structure, meaning they have the highest priority for repayment. Debt investments structured this way are generally considered to be more defensive, since it can protect a BDC from losing all invested capital in a bad transaction.

If an underlying borrower is underperforming and defaults on their loan, they may start to liquidate assets in order to pay down their obligations. In this case, PNNT would be entitled for repayment first since its capital was structured as first lien debt. This increases the probability for PNNT to get some of their capital back. In addition, PNNT has increased their weight in equity-based investments up to 23%. Equity investments don't produce consistent levels of income, but they have the potential to provide a greater amount of capital appreciation if the underlying borrower's valuation increases. Lastly, PNNT's Joint Venture accounts for 6% of the portfolio, but there are signs that this will become a much larger part of the portfolio at some point. When referring to the Joint Venture, management is specifically talking about PennantPark Senior Loan Fund (PSLF).

Additionally, the majority of PNNT's portfolio operates on a floating rate basis. In theory, this should've allowed PNNT to benefit from a higher interest income. A higher interest rate environment directly correlates to a higher interest expense that borrowers have to pay if their debt is structured on a floating rate basis. The downside is that a higher interest rate puts pressure on any portfolio companies that are underperforming, and this can lead to an increased rate of defaults/non-accruals.

Q1 Earnings

PNNT recently reported their Q1 earnings and the result were mixed. Most notably, net investment income landed at $0.20 per share, which was a 10% decrease from the prior quarter's net investment income of $0.22 per share. Additionally, We can see that the fair value of their direct investments have slightly decreased quarter over quarter. However, this was offset by growth in both the Joint Venture Investments and Cash on hand, amounting to $208M and $239M, respectively.

The Joint Venture portion of PNNT's portfolio is becoming a more meaningful piece of the cash flow as management puts emphasis on its growth. For instance, the Joint Venture portfolio is estimated to grow to $1.6B over the year, which can improve PNNT's earnings. The Joint Venture has contributed 18.4% return on invested capital over the last twelve months. From what I can see, this Joint Venture will be the tool that management uses to reduce the overall exposure to equity investments, as they aim to rotate existing equity investments into high-yielding loan assets over 2025.

Additionally, management has allocated $296M towards new investments for the quarter. This was a significant improvement over prior quarters. This was allocated towards 12 new portfolio companies and 61 existing portfolio companies, at a weighted average yield of 10.6%. However, this was likely due to the fact that sales and repayments amounted to $354M, leading to a net decrease of $58M in investment activity for the quarter.

I would like to see an improvement in the net growth in future quarters because there weren't really any quarters where PNNT delivered impactful growth through new investments. An improvement here would ultimately lead to better net investment income performance. The muted net investment income performance resulted in the lack of meaningful NAV growth over the last two years. While a steady NAV is okay, we can argue that the current interest rate environment should have resulted in better results from an increased net investment income.

  • Mar 2023 NAV: $7.60
  • June 2023 NAV: $7.72
  • Sept 2023 NAV: $7.70
  • Dec 2023 NAV: $7.65
  • Mar 2024 NAV: $7.69
  • June 2024 NAV: $7.52
  • Sept 2024 NAV: $7.56
  • Dec 2024 NAV: $7.57

Updated Valuation & Risk Profile

Since PNNT is a business development company, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. AS a result, PNNT's price currently trades at a slight discount to NAV of 5%. Looking over the last decade, the current discount level is actually a lot higher than PNNT's usual range. The price frequently traded at a discount to NAV greater than 20% over the last decade. Just for reference, the price traded at an average discount to NAV of 19% over the last three-year period. I have a bit of trouble determining what justifies the shrinking discount level.

For instance, NAV growth is almost non-existent and net investment income levels haven't risen. While PNNT's total assets have steadily increased, I do not think this warrants the discount level to steadily shrink the way that it has. For instance, non-accruals haven't really improved since the time of my last coverage. When it comes to measuring the risk profile of a BDC, I like to monitor changes in the non-accrual rate over time. Non-accruals represent the amount of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming their original expectations and aren't actively contributing towards a BDC's net investment income levels anymore.

At the time of my last coverage, non-accruals amounted to 4.2% of cost and 2.5% of fair portfolio value. As of the latest earnings call, management confirmed that the non-accrual rate now sits at 4.3% of cost and 1.5% of market value. The non-accruals only amount to 2 different portfolio companies, with the most recent addition being Pragmatic Institute. Management sees some restructuring to take place with the borrowers in non-accrual status.

Our current assumption is that during this quarter, the quarter ended March, there will be some form of restructuring there, some debt will be converted and there'll be a yield instrument coming out of that. - Art Penn, CEO

While non-accruals aren't the entire picture, they definitely have an influence on the future success of a BDC. For me personally, the non-accrual rate can also be an indication of how strong management's underwriting capabilities are when assessing a potential investment. As interest rates remain high, I do not feel confident that the non-accrual rate will significantly improve over 2025, especially since the outlook has shifted. The Fed's original expectation was that there would be roughly four interest rate cuts to happen.

Since inflation has remained stubborn, the Fed has shifted to a more hawkish stance and now only estimate a single interest rate cut for the year. Higher rates will continue to put pressure on the portfolio. Just for a point of reference, here are the non-accrual rates for some peer BDCs:

  • Capital Southwest (CSWC): 2.7% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 0.9% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC): 0.4% non-accrual rate at fair value.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly dividend of $0.08 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13.3%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by 3 to get a quarterly conversion, we land at a quarterly payout amount of $0.24 per share. As previously mentioned, net investment income landed at $0.20 per share for the quarter, which still indicates that its base distribution is not fully covered by net investment income. However, it seems like management is comfortable with this because of the excess of distributable spillover of $0.99 per share.

For the quarter ended December 31st, our GAAP and core net investment income was $0.20 per share, which is $0.04 below our quarterly dividend. PNNT has $65 million, or $0.99 per share of undistributed spillover income. In order to continue to comply with the tax rules, we are required to distribute the spillover income over time. We believe PNNT can generate core NII of $0.21 to $0.22 per share. However, if core NII remained at the current level of $0.20 per share, it would take over 24 quarters or six years to fully distribute the spillover income. - Art Penn, CEO

Management essentially has to use this spillover to fund the dividend. So by this metric, the dividend probably won't be cut in the short term as long as there is excess spillover that needs to be distributed. However, I would like to see management turn some of this spillover into cash by growing their baseline of investments that generate income. In the short term, there's even a possibility that PNNT implements some sort of larger supplemental in order to pay out this spillover balance. It seems to be up in the air right now, but I wouldn't anticipate anything like this happening until later in the year, when management more clearly lays out their investment plans in future quarters.

It's worth mentioning that the distributions received from PNNT aren't the most tax efficient, since they are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends are usually taxed as ordinary income, which isn't ideal when compared to the qualified dividends you'd receive from traditional dividend paying stocks. Based on your specific income needs and amount of capital invested, it may be best to utilize PNNT within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating back to a hold, as the dividend coverage isn't as questionable as it once seemed. PNNT holds two years' worth of undistributed spillover income that it will be using to make up the difference in net investment income. Therefore, the current distribution may be safe in the short term, but this still doesn't take away from the fact that net investment income isn't supporting the base distribution anymore. This takes away from confidence when we consider that the rate of investment activity hasn't been large enough to lead to any meaningful growth in earnings. Additionally, I believe that the valuation is a bit expensive at the moment as non-accruals haven't seen any significant improvements. For now, I plan to stay on the sidelines and continue to monitor changes over the next few quarters.

data.title: Regency Centers: A Strong Business With A Weak Investment Case
data.description:
Jacksonville, Florida-based real estate investment trust ("REIT"), Regency Centers Corporation (NASDAQ:REG), is known as an owner-operator and developer of high-quality grocery-anchored shopping centers that are strategically positioned in affluent suburban trade areas across the country. The company has a long history of operational excellence, possesses a strong business model, many competitive advantages, robust financials, and disciplined investment policies. Nevertheless, the company is grossly overpriced, making it unattractive as an investment, even as it is attractive as a business.

The Business Model

Regency Centers benefits from the long-run fundamentals underpinning neighbourhood and community shopping centers, with its focus on grocery-anchored retail properties. As of the end of 2023, Regency Centers had full or partial ownership interests in 482 properties whose crown jewels are market-leading grocery stores, covering some 56.8 million square feet of gross leasable area ("GLA").

Regency Centers earns around 97% of its revenue from long-term leases with national and regional retailers, with a great focus on necessity-based tenants such as grocery stores, pharmacies, restaurants, and service-oriented businesses.

Regency Centers’ grocery stores and essential retail businesses offering non-discretionary goods and services, ensure consistent foot traffic and tenant stability. Regency Centers manages its portfolio of properties through acquisitions, dispositions, and redevelopment projects, tilting toward high-growth suburban markets with attractive demographic trends. Regency Centers enjoys strong leasing activity, executing more than 2,032 new and renewal leases totalling over 9.9 million Pro-rata square feet ("SF"), with a blended rent spread of 9.5% in 2024, compared to 1,839 new and renewal leases representing 6.9 million Pro-rata SF with a rent spread of 10% in 2023. Regency Centers has a disciplined, prudent approach to new developments and redevelopments, targeting high-yield projects that enhance long-term value.

Strong Financial Performance

Pro-rata same property net operating income ("NOI"), excluding termination fees/expenses, grew 3.1% year-over-year, while the occupancy rate reached a record-high 96.7% leased rate as of the end of 2024, with shop space occupancy reaching 94.1% in that time. Regency Centers’ funds from operations ("FFO") rose from $4.15 per share in 2023 to $4.30 per share in 2024. Core operating earnings rose 5% year-over-year, highlighting the REIT’s solid operational execution.

For comparability across industries, I like to convert earnings to net operating profit after tax ("NOPAT"), and this is revealing here because Regency Centers’ NOPAT is growing much faster than its reported earnings, with NOPAT rising from $248.6 million in 2020 to $547.3 million in 2024, compounding at a rate of 17.1% a year.
